Value: In the pKa values column, pKa values followed by capital letter A have been referred, directly or indirectly, to the same standard and may be compared to one another: A: Complete dissociations of HClO4 and p-toluenesulfonic acid.
Note on the small letter in the Water Content column a: Concentrations of acidic and basic impurities: acids 5.7 x 10-4N and bases 1.0 x 10-4N.
pKa was obtained by using the Fuoss and Kraus equation.
pKa was obtained by using the Shedlovsky method.

pKa was obtained by the overlapping indicator method in solutions containing the acid (or the base) and its conjugate base (or acid), both in ca. 10-2M, and an appropriate indicator (10-5M). HClO4 and p-toluenesulfonic acid were assumed to be completely dissociated.

pKa was determined in the presence of Et4NClO4 from the half-neutralization potential of the acid with 0.1M Bu4NOH (in MeOH-iso-PrOH). The hydrogen electrode was calibrated in dil. HClO4 assuming  its complete dissociation. When pK1and pK2 for a dibasic acid were close to each other, the method of Martin (J. Phys. Chem., 75, 2657 (1971) was employed.
pKa was determined potentiometrically in solutions containing the acid (or the base) and its conjugate base (or acid). HClO4  p-toluenesulfonic acid were assumed to be completely dissociated.

Trace water in DMA was removed by adding sodium bis(methoxy-2-ethoxy)aluminohydride NaAIH2(O-CH2-CH2- OCH3)2. The dehydrated DMA gave a pH range of ca. 45 units, as obtained by voltammetry at a hydrogen electrode. pKa of the acid was obtained by using the standard potential of the hydrogen electrode.

Emfs of two cells, glass electrode/HCl(DMA)/AgCl,Ag and glass electrode/Bu4NDH(DMA)/AgCl,Ag, were measured by varying the concentrations of HCl and Bu4NOH. The results were used to determine the standard emf and pKs.

pKs' values in the presence of water were obtained by pKs' = (Es/59) - log c(HClO4)c(Me4NOH), where Es (in mV)is the difference between the half-neutralization potentials of HClO4 and Me4NOH at concentrations c(HClO4) and c(Me4NOH) at the instant of half-neutralization. pKs was obtained as pKs' extrapolated to zero water content. By a different method of extrapolation to zero water content,  pKs ~ 40 was obtained. See also *6.

In the pKa values column, pKa values followed by capital letter A have been referred, directly or indirectly, to the following standards: A: Complete dissociation of HClO4, pKa(chloroacetic acid) =8.0.
Note on the small letter in the Water Content column:  a Concentration of impurities: acids 2 x 10-4N and bases 2 x 10-4N.
The Fuoss and Kraus equation was used.
pKa was determined from paH in the solutions containing the acid, its conjugate base and 0.5M NaClO4. The hydrogen electrode was calibrated in dil. HClO4 (completely dissociated).
pKa was determined from the half-wave potential for the reduction of the acid at a platinum electrode in 0.5M NaClO4 saturated with hydrogen. pKa(chloroacetic acid)=8.0 was used as reference.

Emfs of two cells, glass electrode/HCl(HMPA)/AgCl,Ag and glass electrode/Bu4NOH(HMPA)/AgCl,Ag were measured by varying the HCl and the Bu4NOH concentrations. The results were used to determine the standard emf and pKs.
In the pKa values column, pKa values followed by capital letter A have been referred, directly or indirectly, to the following standards: A: pKa(HClO4)=4.5, pKa(HPic)=11.0.
Note on a small letter in the Water Content column: a Basic impurity, estimated spectrophotometrically by the addition of HPic, was less than 8 x 10-5 M.
HClO4 in anhydrous acetic acid was diluted with MIBK immediately  before measurements.
pKa was obtained by the Shedlovsky or the Davies method. Kf(HA2-) was obtained by the French and Roe method.

paH values in the mixtures of the acid and its conjugate base (R4N+salt) or the base and its conjugate acid (picrate or perchlorate) were measured, using a glass electrode calibrated in mixtures of HClO4(pKa=4.5) and Bu4NClO4. The effect of incomplete salt dissociation was taken into account in getting pKa from the paH at half-neutralization.
The hydration constants of the proton with 1, 2, 3 and 4 water molecules were determine to be Kf1w= 3 x 10^4, Kf2w= 5 x 10^4, Kf3w= 5 x 10^4, and Kf4w = 3.5 x 10^4.
The glass electrode was calibrated in a 1:2 mixture of HPic(pKa = 11.0) and Et4NPic.
To 100 ml of the 10-5M indicator solution, 0.01M HClO4 was added in small doses. After each addition, the solution was measured potentiometrically as well as spectrophotometrically.
Simultaneous potentiometric and spectrophotometric measurements were carried out during the titration of the indicator with Bu4NOH(in 9:1 benzene-MeOH).

pKa was estimated from the potential at half-neutralization of the base with 0.1M HClO4  of the acid with Bu4NOH (in 9:1 benzene-MeOH). Real pKa values may deviate to some extent from the values in the table because of the incomplete salt dissociation in MIBK.

In the pKa values column, pKa values followed by a same capital letter have been referred, directly or indirectly, to the same standard(s)*.  A: pKa(4-chloro-2,6-dinitrophenol)=4.22, pKa(4,6-dinitro-2-methylphenol) =5.33,  [pKa(benzoic acid)=13.3),  B: Complete dissociation of p-toluenesulfonic acid, pKa(benzoic acid)=12.3,  C: Complete dissociation of HClO4, [pKa(benzoic acid)=10.0).  * Fairly big discrepancies are observed between pKa values reported from different laboratories. This may be caused, in part, by complicating factors, such as homoconjugation and ion-association. The incomplete solvent purification may also cause the disagreements [80B].

Note on the small letters in the Water Content column:  a: Contents of water and other impurities were not reported, though the solvent was carefully purified. b: Concentrations of acidic and basic impurities were 2.1 x 10-3N and 1.6 x 10-3N, respectively.
Complete dissociation of the acid (0.01 Mto 0.002M) was concluded from the Nernstian response of the glass electrode.

The glass electrode was calibrated in solutions of p-tolulenesulfonic acid (completely dissociated). pKa was obtained from paH in HA-Et4NA or in B-BHSO3CH3(1:1) mixture. The HA-Et4NA solution contained 4% benzene and 0.5% MeOH,which were brought in by the titrant Et4NOH(in 9:1 benzene-MeOH).
The indicator electrode was calibrated in solutions of strong acids (HClO4, HI,CH3SO3H)
pKa was obtained from the voltammetric half-wave potential of the acid at a platinized Pt hydrogen electrode.
pKa was obtained by direct spectrophotometric measurements.
pKa's were obtained by the overlapping indicator method starting from HClO4 (completely dissociated).
pKa's were obtained by the overlapping indicator method starting from 4-chloro-2,6-dinitrophenol(pKa = 4.22) and 4,6-dinitro-2-methylphenol(pKa = 5.33).
The influence of ion-association was taken into account.
Values reported in other literatures.
The Fuoss and Kraus equation was used.

Emfs of two cells, glass electrode/HCl(NMP)/AgCl,Ag and glass electrode/Bu4NOH(NMP)/AgCl,Ag, were measured by varying the HCl and the Bu4NOH concentrations. The results were used to determine the standard emf and pKs.

pKa values of the following sterically hindered phenols, bisphenols, and hydroxyphenyl ketones were determined by potentiometry with a glass electrode, using pKa(benzoic acid)=12.3) as reference[73K].

In the pKa values column, pKa values followed by the same capital letter have been referred, directly or indirectly, to the same standard.  A: pKa(1,3-diphenylguanidine)=17.2.  B: pKa(1,3-diphenylguanidine)=14.5.  C: pKa(HPic)=7.94 (see *1c).  D: pKa(HPic)=7.67.

pKa was obtained from the potential at half-neutralization of the base with HClO4. 1,3-Diphenylguanidine(DPhG, pKa = 17.2) served as reference. The linear response of the glass electrode, confirmed in 4 x 10-4 - 4 x 10-5 M HClO4, ,gave pKa(DPhG)=17.2 within the accuracy of ± 0.5.
As in *1, but after making a statistical correction.

As in *1, but pKa(DPhG)=14.5,instead of 17.2 in *1, was used as reference. The difference between the pKa(DPhG)values was due to the correction made in *1b for the incomplete salt dissociation. When pKa values of group A are to be compared with those of group B, this difference must be taken into account.

pKa was obtained from the half-neutralization point using pKa(HPic)=7.94 as reference. The glass electrode was calibrated in picrate buffer solutions. The base titrant was 1,3-diphenylguanidine, which was criticized[79K] not to be strong enough as a base titrant.

pKa was obtained by the titration of the acid with ca. 2.3M (~50%) aq. Bu4NOH, using pKa(DPhG)=17.2 as reference. Water introduced with the titrant had no practical influence on the values of pKa and homoconjugation constant.

In the potentiometric titration of the base with HClO4, two potential jumps were observed at 50% and 100% neutralization points, showing the formation of stable B2H+. pKa and logKf(B2H+) were obtained by pKa = 0.5(pH25 + pH75) and logKf(B2H+) = 0.5(pH25 - pH75) - logC, respectively, where pH25 and pH75 are the pH at 25 and 75% neutralizations and C is the initial concentration of the base.

pKa and Kf(HA2-) were obtained from paH - log(Ca/Cs) relations for the acid and its tetraphenylarsonium salt by the method of Kalthoff and Chantooni [J. Am. Chem. Soc., 87, 4428 (1965)]. pKa was referred to pKa(HPic)=7.67.

Acidic impurity of 5 x 10-6N and basic impurity of less than 1 x 10-6 were contained.

The standard potential of the hydrogen electrode, determined in anhydrous HClO4  solutions, was +0.075 V vs. Ag/0.1M AgClO4(SL) and used as reference. pKa was obtained from the potentials of the hydrogen and/or the glass electrodes in0.01M HA-0.01M Et4Na, assuming the Nemstian response of the electrodes. Benoit and Pichet[73B] obtained -0.10 V vs. Ag/0.1M AgClO4 as the standard potential of the hydrogen electrode. If based on this value, the pKa value should become smaller by ca. 3 units.
The completely hydrated glass electrode was used after calibration in buffer solutions of picric acid (pKa = 17.4) and benzoic acid (pKa = 26.3). The temperature of the solution was not given.

pKa was obtained from the -log[Pic-] vs. -logC plot (C: analytical concentration of HPic). Simple dissociation occurred at C< 0.08 M, but at higher concentrations more complicated equilibria were observed. (cf. P.M.P. Eller and J.A. Caruso, Can. J. Chem., 51, 448 (1973)).
